message:
feat(fnp-python): native recfunctions_drop_fields for flat structured dtypes (br-franken_numpy-hswh)

Same pattern as rename_fields: avoid importing numpy.lib.recfunctions
and own the descriptor-filtering logic in Rust.

1. Normalize drop_names (accept str or list[str]).
2. Walk source.dtype.names, skipping dropped names and any that
   target a nested structured field (those require recursion and
   fall back to recfunctions).
3. Build a new [(name, field_dtype), ...] descriptor from kept
   fields and np.dtype() it.
4. Allocate np.zeros(source.shape, dtype=new_dtype) and copy each
   kept field from the source (`out[name] = source[name]`), which
   matches recfunctions' "new-array-with-surviving-fields" result
   exactly.

Fallback branches: asrecarray=True (record subclass), nested
structured fields, list-of-names that doesn't coerce cleanly, and
the "drop every field" edge case (numpy's surface there varies
across versions).

recfunctions_family_matches_numpy_across_drop_rename_append_merge_and_u2s
passes both drop_fields branches (single name "b" and list ["a", "b"]).
Full suite: 395/395 lib tests green on rch.

hswh recfunctions progress: rename_fields + drop_fields native;
append_fields / merge_arrays / unstructured_to_structured remain
delegated (each has numpy-specific semantics around fill_value /
side-by-side field stacking / 2-D-to-1-D reshaping that don't cleanly
separate from recfunctions' state machine).
